content.text:
Here is a proof by mathematical induction that for every positive integer n,  
 1³ + 2³ + ⋯ + n³  =  (1 + 2 + ⋯ + n)².  

Notation. Let  
 S(n)  := 1³ + 2³ + ⋯ + n³,  
and recall the well‐known formula  
 T(n) := 1 + 2 + ⋯ + n  =  n(n + 1)/2.  

We will show  
 S(n)  =  [T(n)]²  =  [n(n + 1)/2]².  

1. Base case (n = 1).  
  S(1) = 1³ = 1,  
  T(1) = 1, so [T(1)]² = 1² = 1.  
  Hence S(1) = [T(1)]².  

2. Inductive step.  
  Assume that for some k ≥ 1,  
    S(k)  =  [T(k)]²  =  [k(k + 1)/2]².  
  We must show  
    S(k + 1)  =  [T(k + 1)]².  

  Now  
    S(k + 1)  
    = S(k) + (k + 1)³  
    = [k(k + 1)/2]²   +   (k + 1)³       (by the inductive hypothesis)  
    =  (k² (k + 1)²)/4   +   (k + 1)³.  

  Factor out (k + 1)²/4 from the sum:  
    =  (k + 1)²   
        · [k²/4  +  4·(k + 1)/4 ]  
      =  (k + 1)²   
        · [ (k² + 4(k + 1)) / 4 ]  
      =  (k + 1)²   
        · [ (k² + 4k + 4) / 4 ]  
      =  (k + 1)²   
        · [ (k + 2)² / 4 ]  
    =  [ (k + 1)(k + 2) / 2 ]².  

  But (k + 1)(k + 2)/2 = T(k + 1), so we conclude  
    S(k + 1)  =  [T(k + 1)]².  

By the principle of mathematical induction, the formula  
 1³ + 2³ + ⋯ + n³  =  [n(n + 1)/2]²  
holds for all positive integers n.  
Equivalently, the sum of the first n cubes equals the square of the sum of the first n positive integers.
